text: A scientist has won a £75,000 prize for bringing humans closer
to communicating with animals. Dr Julie Elie
at the University of California, Berkeley, won the 2026 Coller-Dolittle prize
for her work translating the calls made by zebra finches.
Her work showed how the birds use 11 main
sounds to tell other birds what they are doing.
The research also showed how the finches used
special personalised calls to recognise each other. Zebra finches are common in
Australia and Indonesia and are sometimes kept as pets.
Dr Elie said she chose to study them because they chirp and twitter a lot,
which helped her collect sounds to decode.
She studied the birds for more than 10 years to record and classify all the noises they made.
She used artificial intelligence (AI) to analyse the sounds that made up different calls,
then tested whether she could produce calls that the birds would understand.
To do this, she gave the birds a button that they could press to play recordings of different calls.
When certain calls were played, she would reward the birds with seeds.
Over time the birds learned which
calls would earn them a reward and ignored the others.
Sometimes the finches made mistakes. This helped
Dr Elie's research because usually the birds confused calls that
meant the same thing rather than those that sounded similar but meant different things.
Dr Elie said this showed
"that they understand the meaning of their call types". The Coller-Dolittle prize is
given to scientists who work on advances in human-animal communication.
Many scientists who entered the prize used AI to help them understand animal language.
Other entries this year included a team that showed how African striped mice
used ultrasonic (very high-pitched) squeaks
to identify themselves.
Another team decoded the language of chimpanzees.
